topic: Performance Analysis and Performance Development Programme
subject: pe

items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: Is a tennis serve a closed or open skill?
items.correct: Closed - performed in a predictable, self-paced environment.
items.misconception: Open - because tennis is a fast-paced sport.
items.why: Students judge by the sport, not the moment. The serve happens at the server's pace with a stationary ball - closed. The return of serve is open. Skill classification depends on the SPECIFIC action's environment.

items.q: How many cervical vertebrae are there?
items.why: It's 7. “12” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Bone adaptation to load. “Lung volumes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Total vertebrae in the human spine?
items.why: Exact answer: 33 vertebrae. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
